turns.text:
Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I’d like to ask a few follow-ups to understand more — feel free to answer whatever applies and skip anything that doesn’t:

- How did you form your expectations (friends/family, media, past experiences, cultural/community norms)?
- Which healthcare professionals did you expect to help (GP, midwife, obstetrician, fertility clinic, nurse, pharmacist, community clinic)?
- What kind of support did you expect (medical tests, fertility advice, lifestyle guidance, emotional support, clear next steps, quick referrals)?
- In reality, who actually helped you first and what did they do? Can you give a specific example or moment that stands out?
- How timely and accessible was the real support (appointment wait times, phone/email access, clinic hours, cost or insurance barriers)?
- Were you given clear information and options, or did you feel confused/left to figure things out on your own?
- How did healthcare providers respond emotionally — were they empathetic, dismissive, rushed, reassuring?
- Were you referred to other services when needed? If so, how well did those referrals work?
- Did your partner or others get included in the conversations if you wanted that?
- Did language, culture, or other personal factors affect the support you received?
- Looking back, what surprised you most about the difference between expectation and reality?
- If you could change one thing about the support you received, what would it be?

If you prefer, answer one question at a time — we can go slowly. Would you like to start by describing what you expected before trying to conceive?

*shifts in seat, looks down, then at husband*

Ah... before... trying to make baby?

*pauses, thinking*

I... not think much. In China, you get pregnant, then... doctor. Just like that.

*makes simple gesture*

Here... different. Husband say... need talk to doctor first. So... I come.

*glances at interviewer uncertainly*

Before trying... I expect... nothing special. You know? Just happen.

*fidgets with hands*

My mama, my sisters... they not talk about this. Just... babies come. Is normal.

*very quietly*

I watch TV here... see many things... but... confusing.

*looks to husband, then back*

What you... what you ask? Too many question. Sorry.

Thank you. One small question:

When you first talked with a doctor here about trying to get pregnant, what surprised you the most — good or bad?  
For example: Did the doctor listen to you? Did they ask many questions? Did they tell you many steps? Did you feel comfortable or worried? Did your husband speak for you like at home in China, or was it different?

Can you tell me one thing that surprised you and how it made you feel?

*looks down, pauses, then glances at husband briefly*

Umm... doctor ask... ask many question. Very much. 

*fidgets with hands*

In China, husband speak. Here... different. Doctor talk... to me. Direct. 

*voice quieter*

Good surprise. But... fast. Very fast talking. No understand all.

*small shrug*

Make feel... okay. But nervous also.

Before you started trying to have a baby, how did you expect you would feel inside — ready, calm, excited, nervous? And when you actually began trying, how did your feelings turn out to be different?

If it helps, can you tell me:
- One feeling you expected, and one feeling you actually felt?
- Did anything about your feelings surprise you?
- Did your feelings change over time, or stay the same?

*looks down, then at husband briefly*

Ah... expected... excited, yes. But...

*pauses, searching for words*

Actually... nervous. Very nervous. More nervous than... than think before.

*fidgets with hands*

Surprise? Yes. Baby come so fast. Not ready inside, you know? Mind not... not prepared like body.

*speaks more softly*

Now... still nervous. But also... happy? Happy and scared same time.

*glances at husband, then back*

Feeling change... yes. First month excited. Now... tired. Worried about... about baby okay. About money. About...

*trails off, uses Mandarin softly*

很多事...

*switches back to English*

Many things worry now.

I’m sorry you felt that way — that sounds really hard. Can you tell me what was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

If it helps, you could tell me:
- A moment when you needed support and didn’t get it.
- How your husband reacted and whether you wanted something different from him.
- Anything you wished a midwife, doctor, or nurse had said or done.
- Whether family or friends were there in the way you needed, or not.

Take your time.

*looks down, fidgets with hands*

Um... support? Hard, yes. Very hard.

*pauses, searching for words*

Husband... he work, work, work. Tired. I say... I say "baby hurt, back hurt" — he say "okay, okay" but... *gestures vaguely* ...not listen, maybe.

*voice quieter*

Doctor... she talk fast. I no understand. She say many thing, I say "yes, yes" but... *taps head* ...nothing stay here. No one... no one slow down. No one say again.

*looks up briefly*

Family in China far far away. Here, no one. No mother, no sister. Husband mother... she not come. 

*wraps arms around self*

One time, I cry in office. Nurse, she say "you okay?" I say yes because... *shrugs* ...what else say? She no sit. No... no ask more. Just go.

*voice trails*

I need... someone explain slow. Someone... *searches* ...someone sit, talk, understand. Is lonely, understand?

*pauses*

Sorry, my English... not good.

Before you tried to get pregnant, when you went online or used apps, what did you expect you would find about fertility and getting pregnant? And when you actually looked, what did you find — was it what you needed?

(Take your time. If it helps, tell me things like: which websites or apps you used, whether the information was easy to understand, if it made you feel better or more worried, or if something important was missing.)

*looks down, shifts in seat*

Online? Um... no look online. *pauses* 

Husband say... say maybe baby come. I not... I not search. No computer good.

*fidgets with hands*

In China, mother say "eat good food, rest." Is enough. Here... different. I see TV show about baby, but... *waves hand vaguely* ...many words, not understand.

*looks at husband, then back*

Apps? No use app. Phone only... WeChat talk family in Fujian. 

*voice quieter*

After pregnant, husband find... find... *searches for words* ...website say what eat, what no eat. But very long, very hard read. I stop after... after page one, two.

*shrugs slightly*

Nothing I need. Doctor here tell me more than... than computer. Computer make worry only. Worry, worry, worry.

*touches belly*

Better just... come appointment. You tell me. More simple.

Okay. Can I ask about that?

What was the biggest difference between what you saw on TV or online and what your doctor or nurse told you? 

For example:
- Was advice about food or rest different?
- Or about when the baby will come, or medicine, or tests?

If you did not look online, is the TV advice different from the doctor? Which do you trust more, and why?

You can answer in short phrases. If you want, I can ask in Chinese.

*looks down, then glances at husband*

TV say... eat много много. Doctor say "no, no." 

*gestures with hands to show "too much"*

Food different. TV lady very fat, okay. Doctor say... dangerous.

*pauses, thinking*

Rest... TV say relax all day. Doctor say walk, walk. Move baby down.

*nods slightly*

Doctor... I trust more. Real person, yes? TV just... show show.

*fidgets with hands*

Medicine also different. TV say take this, take that. Doctor say... maybe only one. Safe for baby.

*looks uncertain*

Sorry, English no good.

Before you became pregnant, did you have any health problem already—like diabetes, high blood pressure, thyroid, or something else?

If yes: what did you expect the doctors or clinic would do to help you get ready for pregnancy? For example, give tests, medicine, advice, or send you to a specialist?

And what actually happened when you tried to get that help? 
- Who did you see? 
- Did they explain things in a way you could understand? 
- Did you get tests or medicine? 
- Was there a long wait, or trouble with cost or language?

If you did not try to get help, can you tell me why not?

(You can tell me a little at a time—if I ask something that does not fit, that is ok.)

*looks down, fidgets with hands*

No... no problem. Healthy before.

*pauses, seems uncertain about the second part of question*

I... not understand "get ready." 

*husband would help explain, but he's not here right now*

No doctor before... before baby. Just... just come here, come America. Then... then pregnant already.

*shifts in seat, gives short answer to avoid more confusion*

No test before. No medicine.

Thank you. It's okay — take your time.

Did you have to ask a lot for help, or did the clinic/doctors call you and give help without you asking? 
For example: did you need to push for appointments, tests, or an interpreter, or did they reach out to schedule and explain things for you?

Can you tell me about one time they helped you, or one time you had to ask for something? It's fine if you don't remember or want your husband to help explain later.

*looks down, fidgets with hands*

Um... husband... he call. He ask appointment.

*pauses, thinking*

Doctor... yes, doctor say come back. Two week. Okay.

*glances up uncertainly*

Interpreter? No... no interpreter. Husband translate. Always husband.

*speaks more quietly*

One time... blood test. I not understand. Husband explain... needle, check baby. Okay.

*hesitates, unsure if answering correctly*

They not... call me. Husband go, talk on phone. I stay home.

*slight worried expression*

Is... is problem?

Before you got pregnant, what did you think your husband would do to help you try to have a baby? For example:
- go with you to the clinic or doctor,
- help with food or remember things mother told you to do,
- stop drinking or smoking, or change work,
- take care of the house or help you rest,
- talk with you about having a baby.

Can you tell me what he actually did? Was it the same as you expected, or different?

(If it helps, you can tell me one thing he did that surprised you. Or your husband can answer if he wants to.)

*shifts in seat, glances at husband*

He... go clinic. Yes, yes. Always come.

*pauses, fidgets with hands*

Food... he remember. My mama say eat ginger, chicken soup. He... he buy. Good.

*looks down*

Work... same same. Construction, very busy. But... come home, help rest. Good husband.

*speaks quietly*

Surprise? He... he talk baby. Before, I think... men no talk. But he talk. Say... say he happy. Very happy.

*nods*

Different good. Better than... before I come here.

Thank you — that helps. I want to ask something gently.

Were there times when you felt most of the preparation fell on you, more than on your husband? Can you tell me about those times and what made it feel that way?

If it helps, you can tell me:
- What kinds of things you were doing (shopping, cleaning, planning).
- Whether his work, money, or family expectations changed things.
- How those moments made you feel, and if anything helped or made it harder.

*shifts in seat, looks down at belly*

Mmm... yes, fell on me.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

I do... shopping. Cleaning. Cook every day. He... work, work, work. Construction. Tired.

*fidgets with hands*

I ask him... baby things. He say "okay, okay" but... not do. I do.

*speaks more quietly*

家里的事... ah, sorry. All house things. I alone.

*glances at husband briefly, then away*

He... money good. But... I carry baby, I work house, I... tired also.

*nods slowly*

Some day... very hard. Swollen feet. But still cook, clean. He sleep after work.

*brief pause*

Is... normal, maybe? I not know.

*looks at interviewer with slight uncertainty*

His family... no see baby yet. Far away. So... just me and him. And I do more.

*voice very soft*

Make feel... alone sometime.

Did you ever have a pregnancy that ended early — like a miscarriage or a stillbirth? 

If yes: When you decided to try again, what did you expect the clinic or doctors to do to help you? For example: run tests, give medicine, do extra check-ups or scans, or talk with you about what happened. What did they actually do for you? 

Can you tell me more — did they explain why it happened, give any medicine, offer counselling, or ask your husband to come? Was it easy to get that help (time, money, travel)? If it’s easier, your husband can help explain. If you didn’t have a previous loss, you can just say “No.”

*shifts in seat, looks down at hands*

No... no happen before. First baby.

*touches belly gently*

This one... okay. Doctor say okay.

*glances toward husband, then back*

Thank you. A quick check: you said this is your first baby, so you haven't had a pregnancy loss before—is that right?

If so, I want to ask about how people have been listening to you now. Have there been times during this pregnancy when you felt the doctors or clinic staff didn’t take your worries or questions seriously?  

Can you tell me about one time, if there was one? How did that make you feel? (It’s okay if there wasn’t—just tell me what it’s been like for you.)

*nods slowly, looking down*

Yes. First baby.

*pauses, shifts in seat*

One time... doctor very fast. I ask about pain—here—*gestures vaguely to lower abdomen* and doctor say "normal, normal." But I... worried. He no listen good.

*fidgets with hands*

Make me... sad. Scared maybe.

*glances at husband, then back down*

I no know if... okay or not okay. So I... quiet.

*soft voice*

My husband there but... he work. I alone many time.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you think your first antenatal visits would be like — the clinic itself, the people there, and the kind of information they would give you? For example, did you expect it to be calm or busy, the doctor to explain tests and what to eat, or for your husband to come with you? And what were the visits actually like?

Can you tell me more about any differences? (Was it noisy or crowded? Did the nurses and doctor speak kindly and explain things? How long did you wait? Were you able to ask questions? Did your husband come?)

*shifts in chair, looks down, speaks quietly*

I think... busy place. Yes. Many people, many waiting.

*pauses, glances at husband*

I think doctor say... eat good food. Baby good. Husband come, yes. He help me... understand.

*fidgets with sleeve*

But actual... very crowded. Very loud noise. Long time waiting. Maybe two hour, three hour.

*speaks more slowly*

The nurse... she fast. She not explain much. The doctor... he okay. He show picture baby on screen. That good.

*looks uncertain*

I ask one time... about pain here. *gestures to lower back* He say... normal. Okay.

*quiet voice*

I think... different from home. In Fujian... smaller place. Quiet. The doctor know you.

*pauses, adds simply*

Here... very busy. Very fast. Husband very help. Without him... I understand nothing.

*nods slightly, then looks away*

It okay. Baby healthy. That important.

Thank you — that helps. You said it was crowded and loud. What was the biggest surprise, good or bad, about those first appointments? For example, did anything about how the staff treated you or how much time they spent with you stand out — something that made you feel cared for or, on the other hand, something that made you feel rushed or not respected?

*shifts in seat, glances at husband briefly*

Biggest surprise? Um... 

*pauses, searching for words*

Doctor... very fast. No time talk much. 

*touches belly absently*

In China, doctor... more time. Ask many thing. Here... five minute, maybe. Finish.

*hesitates, then speaks more quietly*

One nurse... very kind. Smile. Show picture baby on... 超声? 

*looks to husband for help, then back*

Ultrasound. She explain nice. Not rush. That... good.

But many time... staff not look at me. Talk to husband only. I sit there... 

*makes small gesture of invisibility*

Like I not there, you know?

Before you had any screening tests or scans, how did you think they would give you the results? For example: would a doctor tell you, or a nurse? Would they tell you quietly in a private room, or in the waiting area? Would they show you the scan pictures or give you paper? Would your husband be there when they told you?

And then, what actually happened? Who told you the results, where were you, did they explain clearly, did you have time to ask questions, and how did that make you feel?

If it helps, tell me about:
- Whether you got the news right away or had to wait or come back later.
- If the person who told you used simple words you could understand.
- Anything that surprised you or that you wished had been different.

*shifts in seat, glances at husband*

Before? I think... doctor tell. Maybe private room, yes? I no know about pictures.

*pauses, looks uncertain*

My husband... I hope he come. Better, you know? Two person understand more.

*fidgets with hands*

What happen... the ultrasound lady, she show picture on machine. Very fast. She no talk much. Then we wait... wait long time in room. Doctor come after, maybe thirty minute? Forty minute?

*shakes head slightly*

She speak... some big word. I no understand "anterior" or... something. I just say "okay, okay." Husband, he listen better English, but he work that day, so not there.

*looks down*

No paper. No picture to take home. I want picture, but I no ask. She go very fast.

*quietly*

I feel... confused? Not bad, but... confused. I wish more slow. More simple. I ask husband after, "what she say?" But he not there, so...

*shrugs*

Is okay. Baby okay. That is important.

*adds uncertainly*

You... understand me good?

You looked unsure just now. Did the doctor or the ultrasound lady tell you enough for you to understand the results, or was there information missing that you needed to make sense of it?

Can you tell me what things you wished they had explained more? For example, the meaning of the picture, what will happen next, or what the numbers mean?

Would it have helped to have your husband there to explain or ask questions with you?

*looks down, fidgets with hands*

Um... picture... not clear. Doctor say... say baby good, but... *pauses, searching for words* ...numbers, I not understand. What mean "percentile"? 

*speaks more quietly*

Husband... he work. Not there yesterday. When he come, I... I forget what say. *gestures vaguely* 

Maybe... maybe picture explain more? With... with words? *shows uncertainty with shoulder shrug* 

Yes, husband help. He speak better English. He ask question. I... I just say okay. *looks up briefly* But he no there, so...

*trails off, uncertain*

Everything okay with baby? That what... important.
